text: The surgeon demonstrates the port placement for a laparoscopic cholecystectomy, starting with a supraumbilical camera port. An epigastric port is positioned two centimeters below the liver's lower border, directed towards the gallbladder. Additional assisting ports are placed in the midclavicular and midaxillary lines, ensuring ergonomic triangulation for gallbladder manipulation. The ports are spaced six to eight centimeters apart, facilitating stress-free instrument handling.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis on the gallbladder, carefully separating flimsy adhesions while noting the proximity of the duodenum. Traction is applied to safely mobilize the gallbladder, ensuring the duodenum is pushed away. The peritoneal attachments are divided, revealing the Ruvius sulcus in the background. The surgeon scores the peritoneum close to the gallbladder, avoiding the CBD and hepatic artery, and begins to visualize the cystic artery, working medial to it.

The surgeon performs peritoneal scoring medial to the cystic artery, removing fatty tissue between the artery and duct. Short bursts of cautery open the avascular plane for safe dissection. The lower end of the gallbladder is mobilized from the cystic plate, with small vessels coagulated using a hook or bipolar. The dissection progresses in the avascular plane near the liver bed, ensuring a comprehensive critical view of safety. The cystic artery is elongated for secure hemostasis, and fatty tissue near the gallbladder is removed with a monopolar hook, creating a window to the posterior side, revealing the liver.

The surgeon uses a hook to divide fatty tissue near the vessel, widening the dissection window. Gradually, the peritoneum is scored, revealing an avascular plane for safe dissection. Two structures leading to the gallbladder are visible, but further dissection is needed for a comprehensive critical view. The surgeon releases more peritoneum on the anterior side, ensuring the porta is away from the dissection area, leaving the gallbladder attached to the cystic plate.

The surgeon uses an atraumatic instrument to milk the cystic duct, ensuring any small stones are pushed back into the gallbladder. A large clip is applied to ligate the cystic duct, followed by suturing with a vial suture to secure the knot and prevent bile spillage. The duct is divided, falling laterally and downwards, indicating it is not the CBD. The gallbladder is released from the cystic plate, with traction revealing the correct dissection planes and releasing flimsy attachments without entering the liver capsule.

The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to dissect flimsy attachments around the gallbladder, maintaining clear visibility by cleaning the hook tip to reduce fumes. Small vessels are coagulated with the hook, and traction is adjusted to identify dissection planes. The posterior portion of the gallbladder is released, ensuring hemostasis in the liver bed. The gallbladder is placed in an endo bag, bile is suctioned out, and the gallbladder is collapsed before removal.

The surgeon closes the tenamen ports using a port suture needle and administers a laparoscopic-guided TAP block, with the transversus abdominis muscle visibly bulging. The critical view of safety is established, showing the cystic duct and artery clearly from the anterior view, with small peritoneal attachments being dissected. The gallbladder is positioned higher than the porta, confirmed by the doublet view from both anterior and posterior perspectives. The Ruvius sulcus is visible, indicating the safe dissection zone above it, avoiding the CBD below. The surgeon scores the peritoneum close to the gallbladder, correcting the dissection line to stay above the cystic lymph node, ensuring ligation of the artery above this landmark.

The surgeon continues dissection in the correct plane, revealing a cleavage plane between the artery and gallbladder. The Ruvius sulcus is visible on the left side of the screen, guiding the dissection. The surgeon releases small attachments around the gallbladder cystic duct junction, which is positioned higher. A tubular structure is visible, identified as the portal vein, with the hepatic artery retracted by the right instrument and the common bile duct (CBD) on the left side.

The surgeon meticulously dissects around the gallbladder, identifying the proximal course of the hepatic duct and the cystic duct. The hepatic artery is visible, running below the instrument, while small strands are coagulated. The gallbladder is surrounded by significant fat and inflammatory fluid, which is carefully released using a monopolar hook to expose the gallbladder and its attachments to the cystic plate.

The surgeon creates a window around the cystic duct, carefully dissecting small attachments strand by strand. The artery is visible, and a window is opened around it as well. The elongated gallbladder is held midway, revealing only one structure entering due to the wide duct. Intracorporeal sutures are used to ligate the duct proximally, leaving a small cuff of tissue to prevent slippage. Sludge is visible in the duct area, necessitating milking to push remnants into the gallbladder. Sharp and blunt dissection techniques are employed to identify tissue planes, and the gallbladder is removed with hemostasis secured using a bipolar instrument.

The surgeon uses a Maryland bipolar instrument for a combination of sharp and blunt dissection, coagulating small vessels to minimize thermal spread. A window is opened, revealing two structures: the artery and the cystic duct. The surgeon releases the lower end of the gallbladder from the cystic plate to establish the critical view of safety. The artery and cystic duct are clearly visible, with the CBD at a lower level. The gallbladder is densely adherent to the liver bed, prompting a fundus-first technique to find a better dissection plane. The gallbladder is thick-walled and distended, requiring puncture and suction of inflammatory fluid. Adhesiolysis is performed due to previous laparotomy, and ducts are identified and isolated.

The surgeon encounters a pulsatile bleed from the partially coagulated cystic artery, applying gauze pressure to control the bleeding and suctioning the surrounding blood for clear visibility. The gallbladder is released from the liver bed, revealing a stone due to accidental entry into the gallbladder. The surgeon performs sharp and blunt dissection to peel off the gallbladder, noting the thick peritoneal attachments and severe inflammation. Care is taken to avoid entering the liver parenchyma, and inflammatory adhesions are separated using blunt dissection with a suction apparatus.

The surgeon encounters severe inflammation near the gallbladder, with the omentum adhered to the area. A large vessel is used as a landmark for adhesiolysis, working above and lateral to it. A wide cystic duct is ligated, and an intraoperative cholangiogram is performed using a catheter to ensure no stones block the duct. The dye flows clearly through the intrahepatic and CBD, visible on the C-arm, with spillage into the duodenum. The cystic duct junction is divided, and an endo loop is applied to both proximal and distal areas. Dense fibrotic attachments are present, and pus is observed. Despite challenges, the gallbladder is thickened but manageable with laparoscopic techniques. Adhesions due to acute cholecystitis are separated using atraumatic dissection and suction. A puncture needle aspirates gallbladder contents, and a cuff of tissue is left at the gallbladder-cystic duct junction. The gallbladder is incised using a monopolar hook, with a plane created between the gallbladder and cystic plate for careful release of adhesions.

The surgeon releases the last attachments of the gallbladder, disconnecting it at the fundus level to ensure a safe cholecystectomy without bile duct injury. The sloughed-off tissue inside the gallbladder is visible, and the infected material in the liver bed is noted. The gallbladder is used for traction during suturing of the small remnant segment, which is less than one centimeter. A varus needle is used to aspirate the gallbladder, causing it to collapse, allowing for easier manipulation of the thick wall.

The surgeon releases adhesions starting from the cystic duct, tracing it inferiorly towards the duodenum. The common bile duct (CBD) becomes visible, showing distension due to obstructing stones. Using cautery, the surgeon meticulously dissects fibrotic attachments, exposing the gallbladder up to the duodenum. A trans-cystic flushing is attempted, with a balloon moving into the dilated duct, but no major stones are visualized. A choledochotomy is performed, with sharp dissection avoiding electrocautery, and sludge is flushed out using a balloon.

The surgeon places an infant feeding tube as a stent within the choledochotomy site on the dilated common bile duct (CBD) and sutures it in a continuous fashion. A choledochoduodenostomy is performed by making linear incisions along the CBD and duodenum, followed by precise suturing using monofilament PDS sutures to ensure a wide open anastomosis. The posterior and anterior bites are taken carefully to avoid involving the posterior wall.

The surgeon applies an endo loop at the junction of the gallbladder and choledochal cyst, ensuring clear placement. Bleeding from the liver and inflammatory area is addressed using gauze and abgel, effectively stopping the ooze. During CBD exploration, dense adhesions lead to inadvertent duodenal injury, with bile leakage observed. A balloon is inserted through the trans-cystic area, emerging through the injured duodenum, followed by a thorough wash of the area.

The surgeon inserts a double J stent into the common bile duct (CBD) and applies a clip over it. Using a PDS suture, the surgeon sutures the opening of the CBD. The omentum is used for a small pexy. The adhesions are fibrotic, and the gallbladder appears as a thimble gallbladder due to obstructive jaundice. The surgeon performs a subtotal cholecystectomy, dissecting the CBD and releasing the gallbladder completely. A small rent in the CBD is sutured under vision with multiple interrupted sutures. The posterior wall of the gallbladder is left intact, and the rest is removed. A dry gauze is used to check for bile leaks, confirming none are present.
